title: The Frame Returned to Stone
recap:
Deep in the voreth-ravine, Ming Liang set down seventeen years of carried weight — the name-token, the third text, and the frame itself — pressing them into the Fault-vein beside the column with the precision of a man returning borrowed tools. What remained was not a door, not a key, but a harmony: the Wellspring's true frequency, spoken true at the closing. He rose lighter in the only way that word applies to such a thing, acknowledged Orrath as peer with a spear-master's bow, and began the descent with two people who had each, in their own way, held the same instruction from different sides of the same text. Davan was released on the mountain's terms to carry sorveth-tavan to the Thornwood bands before news could outpace the frame; she left without ceremony, as a continuation. What Orrath named on the path down sat heavier than any of it: a circuit closed around the wrong shape does not hold — it cuts. And a Wellspring that is cut does not die slowly. It opens the Fault-scars. Veranthos has forty-four days on Thessaly Vorn's calendar. It may have closer to four on the Wellspring's.

The staging camp glows ahead in lantern-blue, its canvas shapes emerging from mountain shadow as the afternoon light fails. Inside it, four shrine-components sit in their warding seals, and a woman who has been load-bearing her certainty for eleven years believes the geometry is complete. Orrath has spoken plainly: taking a component closes every door; showing Vorn a demonstrable error might leave them open — but neither man yet knows how to engineer the showing. Ming Liang stands at the edge of the camp's sight-line with a quiet Shard at his sternum, a three-source convergence in his body rather than his hands, and the full knowledge of what a wrong ritual will cost. The conversation that has not yet been begun is forty yards away.
turningPoint: Orrath names what a cut Wellspring does to Fault-scar country — not a slow Sundering but a violent inward collapse, Veranthos and the foothills bands measured in days, not weeks — and the staging camp appears through the failing light ahead, its lantern-blue already burning, four shrine-components sealed inside and Thessaly Vorn's certainty intact.

narrativeTurns.playerInput: Say nothing for a moment — hold 'the one who built the frame around the door' and let the weight of Sorveth's choice settle before responding
narrativeTurns.narration:
The one who built the frame around the door.

You hold it. You do not arrange it. The ravine floor, the pale granite, the voreth-column turning behind you, Orrath and Davan present and not pressing — all of it recedes to the periphery while the weight of Liang Sorveth's choice settles into the body the way the Wellspring's grief settled: not as thought but as fact, arriving in the register below thought where the root stance lives.

He knew the word. He knew it was Thornwood. He had reached the source — the older knowledge that the Conclave had never found and the Quietists had never held — and he had read what waited at the fifth-point and understood the distinction between sorveth-anak and the one who dies, and he had made a decision. He built the frame. He hid it in a margin in characters smaller than ornamentation. He lodged the Shard behind the sternum of a student who had not yet been asked, and he sent that student east into exile with the frame and without the answer, and he wrote on the outside of the text: do not let them use you as a key.

Not a warning against the Conclave. A warning against himself — against what he had already done, which was give someone else's body to a geometry they could not yet read. The name-token in your pocket. The teacher who did not come. The seventeen years of a form built around a Shard whose purpose you did not know, built honestly, because the body builds honestly even when the mind is kept uninformed. He gave you the frame and withheld the answer because a sorveth-anak who arrives at the fifth-point from instruction is not testimony. Testimony cannot be taught. It can only be lived into, slowly, from the inside.

The weight of it is not anger. It is heavier than anger and also cleaner — the specific gravity of a thing that cannot be undone and was perhaps the only way, and was still a choice made in someone else's body, and the person who made it named himself after what he had done: the one who built the frame around the door. He has been carrying that name for seventeen years too. You do not know yet what you will say to it. But the weight has settled now. The body knows what it holds.
suggestedActions: Speak — tell Orrath and Davan plainly what Sorveth's choice means to you now that the frame and the answer have both arrived
Ask Davan whether sorveth-anak must go to the fifth-point alone, or whether the witnesses travel with them into the circuit
Ask Orrath what the Unsutured believe happens to the refused shapes — the dreaming — after the Wellspring is sealed as utharvel
Take out the carved name-token and hold it — let Sorveth's name sit in the palm before deciding anything further
Say nothing more here — the ravine has given what it can; begin the return and decide on the road what to bring to Vorn

narrativeTurns.playerInput: I buried the name token and the third text close to the column. This is the answer that the master needs. I never follow any given path, a frame only limits the possibility and the vision. There's no frame, there's no door, there's no key. All we need is a harmony that resonate the Willspring.
narrativeTurns.narration:
You kneel at the ravine floor beside the voreth-column's edge and press two things into the cold granite — not burying them in grief, not in ceremony, but with the practical precision of a man returning borrowed tools to the place they were always meant to end. The name-token first, dark wood against pale stone, pressed into a crack in the Fault-vein until the amber light closes gently around it. Then the third text, folded to its smallest shape, tucked beneath a flat stone beside it. The column's warmth touches your hands as you work. Not approval. Recognition — the same quality as the bridge, the same quality as the mountain's count. These things have come home.

You stand. Not unburdened — lighter is the wrong word for what happens when you set down something you have carried for seventeen years. The word is cleaner. The frame is in the rock. The door was always a fiction of the people who needed a door. Liang Sorveth built the frame because he was a man who needed frames, who thought in thresholds and keys and margins and hidden characters. He was not wrong — he was Sorveth, and the frame served: it got you here, to this ravine, to this column, to the full transmission and the stilled light and the weight you have just set down. You do not owe him anger. You do not owe him forgiveness. You owe him what the Wellspring willed for its refused shapes — to be witnessed as what he actually was. A teacher who loved something and did not know how to give it without a container. You received it. You are done with the container.

The Shard is quiet at your sternum. And from that stillness, without transmission, without reaching: you understand what sorveth-anak actually requires. Not a man carrying a text to the fifth-point. A harmony. The Wellspring's actual shape — utharvel, third thing, grief and dreaming and gladness woven into something that has been itself in the dark for a thousand years — resonating outward from the fifth-point into the re-binding geometry, the way the spear's harmonic resonated through the column when the form was done honestly. The Sealers will try to bind something they have a text for. You will be at the fifth-point speaking what is true.

You turn to Orrath and Davan. Orrath is watching the place where you buried the token and the text, his face holding something released — not the named-patience, not the eleven-year weight. Davan has her eyes on your sternum, reading the Shard's stillness. You speak plainly: 'The frame is in the ground. What remains is the harmony. The fifth-point does not need a door. It needs the Wellspring's true frequency spoken into the geometry at the moment the Sealers close it.' A pause. 'I need to be at the ritual. Not as their fifth component. As the one who speaks what is true at the closing. Thessaly Vorn does not know this distinction exists.' You look at them both. 'We have forty-four days and a conversation that will be very difficult to begin.'
suggestedActions: Ask Orrath and Davan whether one of them can attend the Conclave ritual as Thornwood witness — testimony requires a witness
Begin the descent immediately; forty-four days is enough only if the conversation with Vorn starts soon
Sit with Orrath and Davan here at the ravine floor and let them respond before moving
Ask Davan whether sorveth-anak's harmony can be prepared — whether there is a Thornwood method for holding the Wellspring's frequency clearly enough to speak it into the geometry
Take one last look at the buried token and text before turning toward Veranthos

sceneImage.prompt: A weathered spear master kneels at the base of a slow-turning column of pale voreth-light in a granite mountain ravine, pressing a small dark wooden token and a folded paper into a glowing amber Fault-vein in the rock. His expression is not grief — it is the precise calm of returning something borrowed. Two Thornwood shamans stand witness behind him in the cold mountain air. Characters depicted — Ming (Lean Far-Eastern man, weathered, dark eyes, foreign-cut travelling coat, spear-callus on the right palm but no weapon; moves with the economy of a martial discipline.); Orrath (Old as mapped terrain, face weathered deep, Thornwood clan-braid, heavy woven cloak, tends fire with a short stick); Davan (Young Thornwood woman, hill-country braid, deliberate stillness, heavy-woven cloak). Illustration for a High Fantasy story (Epic, urgent, morally complex — heroism is possible but costly). Cinematic, atmospheric, consistent character appearance.

narrativeTurns.playerInput: Sit with Orrath and Davan here at the ravine floor and let them respond before moving
narrativeTurns.narration:
You sit on the ravine floor. Not cross-legged in formal posture — simply down, back against the cold granite, the voreth-column turning behind you at a distance that is no longer overwhelming. The body has done something enormous and needs to be horizontal to the ground for a moment. The Shard is quiet. The granite is cold through your coat. You let it be cold.

Orrath sits without being invited, which is correct. He does not choose a position that signals anything — he simply finds the rock that will hold him and settles onto it with the unhurried quality of a man who has been sitting on mountain stone his whole life. He is quiet for a long time. Then: 'The Unsutured held the distinction.' He speaks to the middle distance, not to you, the way a man speaks when he is confirming something to himself as much as to a listener. 'Sorveth-anak. We held it because we could not afford to collapse it. If the one who speaks-true and the one who dies are the same word —' A pause. 'Then the Wellspring receives a death at its closing. Not a testimony. A death.' He stops. That is the whole of what he has to say, and it is enough — the eleven years compressed into two sentences.

Davan does not sit immediately. She stands at the column's edge for a moment longer, facing it, the way she faced the Greyveil threshold before stepping onto it — reading what is there before committing weight. Then she comes and sits a little apart from both of you, her knees drawn up, forearms resting across them. She looks at her own hands. Then she looks at you, direct and without preamble. 'The name Sorveth,' she says quietly. 'He carried it his whole life. The one who built the frame around the door.' Her rough-consonant trade-tongue is careful around the edges of this. 'In Thornwood, that name is not a condemnation. It is a description of a kind of love. A person who knows the door must be found and cannot lead you through it.' She is quiet for a moment. 'He sent you with everything you needed and nothing you did not. The frame held.' She does not say this to comfort you. She says it the way she gives technical information — precisely, because it is true and you have earned the truth.

The ravine holds all three of you in the voreth-light. Above, the column turns. Somewhere very far up the slope the staging camp waits, and the King's Road, and forty-four days, and a conversation with Thessaly Vorn that has not yet been begun. But that is above. Here, for this moment, there is cold granite and two people who walked to the end of an eleven-year question and found it answered.
suggestedActions: Ask Orrath directly: when you return to Veranthos, will the Unsutured stand with you at the Conclave — witnesses to the Wellspring's will, not only yours?
Ask Davan whether 'the frame held' means Sorveth knew the testimony would complete — or only that he trusted you to find it
Sit in silence a little longer before speaking — let the mountain hold this moment without making it useful yet
Begin planning the descent — ask Orrath and Davan how they read the return journey and what they carry back to the staging camp that they did not carry up

sceneImage.prompt: Three figures seated on pale granite at the floor of a deep mountain ravine, a slow-turning column of voreth-light — an undecided colour between silver and gold — rising behind them. A spear rests across the knees of the central figure. The two shamans sit apart, facing inward. Cold midday light. The ravine walls close and high. Characters depicted — Ming (Lean Far-Eastern man, weathered, dark eyes, foreign-cut travelling coat, spear-callus on the right palm but no weapon; moves with the economy of a martial discipline.); Orrath (Old as mapped terrain, face weathered deep, Thornwood clan-braid, heavy woven cloak, tends fire with a short stick); Davan (Young Thornwood woman, hill-country braid, deliberate stillness, heavy-woven cloak). Illustration for a High Fantasy story (Epic, urgent, morally complex — heroism is possible but costly). Cinematic, atmospheric, consistent character appearance.

narrativeTurns.playerInput: Begin planning the descent — ask Orrath and Davan how they read the return journey and what they carry back to the staging camp that they did not carry up
narrativeTurns.narration:
The question sits in the ravine air between the three of you, practical and necessary and almost strange after everything that preceded it. The return. What you carry back down.

Orrath is quiet for a moment — not the deliberating silence, the naming silence. He turns the question over the way he turns every question: from the outside first, then from the inside. Then he speaks, in his careful weighted trade-tongue, eyes on the middle distance between the Fault-veins and the column. 'We carry a convergence.' He pauses. 'Three sources. Sorveth's text. The Unsutured position. The Wellspring's own declaration through the column.' He looks at you directly, which he does rarely enough that it lands. 'A single source — Thessaly Vorn dismisses. Two sources — she argues translation. Three sources from three roads that did not know each other.' Something that is not quite satisfaction moves through his face. 'That is not argument. That is geography. The territory is the same from every direction.' He stops there — the shaman's economy, the thing said completely and not extended past its completion.

Davan has been turning a small piece of flat granite over in her fingers — a practitioner's grounding habit, you have come to recognise it. She does not look up immediately when Orrath finishes. Then: 'I carry the word sorveth-anak back into Thornwood.' Her rough-consonant trade-tongue is careful around this, the way it is careful when she is choosing precision over ease. 'The Unsutured have held the distinction for eleven years without a name for the one who carries it. Now there is a name.' A pause. 'A face for the name.' She does glance up then — briefly, at your sternum, the involuntary check. 'The Thornwood bands in the foothills will want to know the Opened Ear received a witness. That the column stilled.' She sets the stone down flat on the granite beside her, deliberately. 'They will want to know the Wellspring is not dying. It is arriving.'

The column turns behind you. The Fault-veins in the ravine walls pulse at their slow amber frequency. Below, the staging camp waits with its canvas and lantern-blue and the cartographer who could not map the ravine. Above Veranthos, forty-four days on a clock that does not know what the three of you are carrying back down the mountain.
narrativeTurns.ask: How do you want to approach the descent and what, if anything, do you settle between the three of you before moving — about Thessaly Vorn, about the Thornwood bands Davan mentioned, or about the question the column left open?
suggestedActions: Ask Davan what the Thornwood bands in the foothills will actually do with the news — whether they are a resource or a complication when you reach Vorn
Ask Orrath directly how he proposes to present three-source convergence to Thessaly Vorn — he has held this position eleven years; he will have thought about the argument
Settle the order of urgency before descending: the Conclave first, or establish Thornwood backing before approaching Vorn
Begin the descent now — let the planning wait until you are off the mountain and can speak at the staging camp with food and fire
Ask Orrath what he recognised at the High Approach when the mountain opened — he made a sound that was not surprise but recognition; he has not yet explained it

narrativeTurns.playerInput: Ask Orrath what he recognised at the High Approach when the mountain opened — he made a sound that was not surprise but recognition; he has not yet explained it
narrativeTurns.narration:
The question has been waiting since the High Approach — that involuntary sound Orrath made when the mountain opened to Ming's words, painful and not surprised, the sound of a man who has arrived at something he already knew the shape of. You ask it plainly, sitting on the ravine floor with the column turning behind you and forty-four days ahead.

Orrath is quiet for a long time. Not the deliberating silence — the finding-the-right-container silence, the one he uses when the thing he needs to say has been sitting in Thornwood for longer than trade-tongue has had a word for it. His eyes move to the granite between his feet, then to the Fault-vein running amber along the ravine wall, then back to you. When he speaks, it is slowly, with the precision of a man translating not between languages but between registers of knowing.

'In the Unsutured oral line —' He stops. Begins again differently. 'We have a record. Older than the Covenant. A name in it we do not speak aloud often because the speaking invites the question of whether we are ready to receive the answer.' A pause. His hands, which have been still since he sat, move once — not a gesture, a grounding. 'The record says: the vessel that carries the Prior Shard will not know it carries anything. It will build its life around the weight without naming the weight. It will be exiled from where it belongs. It will arrive at the column speaking the language of its first home.' He meets your eyes directly, which he rarely does, and holds them. 'The language. Not trade-tongue. Not Avelornian. The first language.' Another pause, shorter. 'I heard Zhennian at the High Approach and the mountain opened and I knew the record was not a story.' He stops there. Something in his face that is not peace and is not grief and is old — the face of a man who has held a prophecy-shaped thing for eleven years knowing he would recognize its completion and not knowing how it would feel to actually do so.

Davan, to your right, has gone very still. She is looking at Orrath with an expression you have not seen from her before — not surprise. The look of someone learning that the person they already trusted knew more than they said, and that the withholding was not betrayal but a different kind of care. The same word, in the end, as Sorveth. She does not speak. She lets Orrath's words stand in the ravine air.

Orrath says, quieter: 'I did not tell you on the road because the record also says the vessel must not be led. Must not know the door until it has already walked through.' He is not apologizing. He is finishing the account precisely. 'You and Sorveth were given the same instruction from different sides of the same text.' A pause. 'I did not know that was possible until this morning.'
narrativeTurns.ask: What do you say to Orrath?
suggestedActions: Say nothing — let the weight of the parallel settle the way you let Sorveth's choice settle. Orrath has earned the same silence.
Ask him to name the record — its source, its age, whether the Conclave or Vorn have any access to it.
Tell Orrath the withholding was correct: you could not have answered the bridge or the column from instruction. Ask what the record says happens after the testimony.
Ask Davan directly whether she knew — whether Orrath carried this alone or whether the two of them held it together on the road.
Stand and begin the descent — receive what Orrath has given without arranging it, let the walk back be the response.

sceneImage.prompt: Three figures sit on pale granite at the floor of a deep mountain ravine, a slow-turning column of voreth light glowing behind them. An old shaman speaks with eyes cast down, hands flat on stone, finally releasing something he has carried alone for eleven years. Amber Fault-veins pulse in the ravine walls around them. Characters depicted — Ming (Lean Far-Eastern man, weathered, dark eyes, foreign-cut travelling coat, spear-callus on the right palm but no weapon; moves with the economy of a martial discipline.); Orrath (Old as mapped terrain, face weathered deep, Thornwood clan-braid, heavy woven cloak, tends fire with a short stick); Davan (Young Thornwood woman, hill-country braid, deliberate stillness, heavy-woven cloak). Illustration for a High Fantasy story (Epic, urgent, morally complex — heroism is possible but costly). Cinematic, atmospheric, consistent character appearance.

narrativeTurns.playerInput: Say nothing — let the weight of the parallel settle the way you let Sorveth's choice settle. Orrath has earned the same silence.
narrativeTurns.narration:
You give Orrath the same silence you gave Sorveth's choice. Not the silence of a man who has nothing to say — the silence of a man who knows that what has just been named is too large for the immediate word, and that the other person in the conversation knows this, and that the knowing is itself the response.

Orrath receives it. You watch something in him settle — not relief, not vindication, but the specific quality of a burden correctly distributed at last. He has carried the record alone for eleven years. He carried it on the road without speaking it. He carried it through the Greyveil threshold and up the High Approach and to the edge of the column while you walked into it. He has now spoken it aloud and the person it describes has heard it and returned him silence instead of demand or gratitude or recrimination, and that is precisely the response the weight required. His hands, resting on his knees, open slightly. A small thing. You notice it.

Davan has been watching you both. She does not speak either. The three of you sit in the ravine with the voreth-column turning behind you and the amber Fault-veins pulsing slow in the granite walls and the cold pressing in from the upper peaks, and the silence holds the shape of what Orrath said — two sides of the same instruction, two people who never knew the other existed, a frame built from the east and a record held in the west and a third thing standing in between that arrived at the column in seventeen years of exile speaking Zhennian to a mountain.

After a long time — long enough that the shadows in the ravine have shifted a fraction, the sun continuing its indifferent arc above the peaks — Orrath says, without preamble, very quietly: 'We should begin the descent before the light changes.' It is practical and it is also the right way to end a silence: with the next necessary thing, not with a word that tries to hold more than silence can.
suggestedActions: Rise, take the lead on the descent back toward the Opened Ear shelf — the Shard has done its work; trust the body now
Before rising, ask Orrath whether the Thornwood bands in the foothills know to expect you — and whether their reception will be straightforward
Ask Davan how she intends to carry the word sorveth-anak back to the Thornwood bands — aloud, in ceremony, or in private council
Let Davan take the rear on the descent — she has crossed more Fault-thresholds than either of you and should read the mountain's register on the way down
Acknowledge Orrath with the spear-master's small bow before rising — the one that names a peer — then begin the descent without further words

narrativeTurns.playerInput: Acknowledge Orrath with the spear-master's small bow before rising — the one that names a peer — then begin the descent without further words
narrativeTurns.narration:
You rise from the granite floor in a single clean movement and turn to Orrath. The bow is small — not a student's bow, not a supplicant's bow. The spear-master's bow: spine straight, head inclined precisely enough to name what is named, no further. The bow that says: you carried something I could not have carried for myself, and I know this, and it will not be forgotten. Orrath receives it without moving. His eyes hold yours through the full duration of it. That is the correct response. That is how a peer receives a peer's acknowledgment — completely, without deflection.

Then you turn and begin the descent.

The Shard is quiet at your sternum. Not oriented now — arrived. The ravine floor gives way to the first ledge and the body reads the rock correctly without consulting the mind: weight left, soft knee, the Fault-vein crossed at its narrowest point. The same sequence as the descent into the ravine, but different in register — then the body was following something forward; now it is simply moving through familiar terrain that happens to be the world. The voreth-column turns behind you as you climb. You do not look back. It does not require looking back. It will be there when it needs to be.

Orrath falls in behind you without instruction, his footfall slow and reading-the-ground as always. Davan takes the outside line, moving with the native ease of someone for whom this quality of terrain is a first language. The three of you ascend in the same earned silence of the climb down, but it carries different weight now — not the silence of people preparing for something, but the silence of people who have done the thing and are letting it settle into the body at the pace the body requires. The staging camp is above. The King's Road is above. Thessaly Vorn and forty-four days and a conversation that has not yet begun are above. For now there is cold granite and the sound of three sets of boots finding their footing, and the amber Fault-veins pulsing warm in the ravine walls as you rise past them, and the mountain receiving your departure the same way it received your arrival — as a continuation.
narrativeTurns.ask: The ravine gives way to the first open slope. The staging camp is visible in the far distance below the High Approach shelf — canvas and lantern-blue, small against the mountain. What do you do on the ascent?
suggestedActions: Climb in silence — let the body work and the mind arrive at the staging camp empty, ready for whatever waits there
Ask Davan as you climb what she knows of the Thornwood bands in the foothills — their numbers, their disposition toward the Conclave, how quickly word of the column's stilling will reach them
Ask Orrath whether the Unsutured oral record names a timeframe — whether it says anything about the forty-four days or the Sundering Clock
Stop at the Opened Ear shelf when you reach it — mark the place in some small way before leaving it behind
Think through what you will say to Thessaly Vorn — begin building the geography of the argument in your mind while your hands and feet are occupied with the climb

narrativeTurns.playerInput: Ask Davan as you climb what she knows of the Thornwood bands in the foothills — their numbers, their disposition toward the Conclave, how quickly word of the column's stilling will reach them
narrativeTurns.narration:
The question finds its moment naturally — a level stretch of path where the ravine's upper wall opens enough that two can walk nearly abreast, your breath steady, the Fault-veins thinning as you climb toward the High Approach shelf above. You ask Davan without breaking stride, the practical register, the way you ask about terrain ahead.

Davan moves for several paces before answering. Not withholding — reading. Her eyes go briefly to the rock face to the left, where a thin Fault-vein pulses amber, then she speaks in her rough-consonant trade-tongue, precise and without ceremony.

'Three bands. Foothills between the eastern scarp and Helvrath's Cross. Together — perhaps sixty, seventy people. Not all fighters.' A pause, footfall on loose scree, weight correctly placed. 'The Conclave turned them back two seasons ago when they tried to send a delegation toward the upper approaches. Wardens at the foothills boundary. No violence — the Sealers are not stupid enough for that — but the message was clear. The mountain is Conclave territory until the Clock resolves.' Her jaw tightens slightly at this, the only external sign. 'The bands do not love Thessaly Vorn. They are not Quietists either — they do not want the Wellspring broken open. They want it witnessed. They have been waiting in the foothills with nowhere to go.' She glances at you once, direct, then returns her eyes to the path. 'Word of the column stilling — I carry it on foot when we descend. The bands have runners between them. Two days, perhaps three, before all three know.'

She is quiet for a moment. Then, unprompted, in a slightly different register — still precise but with a weight under it: 'When they hear the Opened Ear received a witness and the column stilled, they will want to move. Toward the Conclave ritual site. Toward the fifth-point.' She looks at you again, and this time she holds it longer. 'Sixty people arriving uninvited at a Conclave working is not a resource. It is a complication. Unless there is a frame for what they are doing there.' She does not say: you will need to decide whether to give them one. She does not need to. The statement sits in the mountain air between you, complete.
narrativeTurns.ask: The Thornwood bands are two to three days from knowing — and Davan is telling you, plainly, that their movement needs a frame or it becomes a liability. What do you do?
suggestedActions: Ask Davan whether the bands would accept a specific role — witnesses at the boundary of the ritual, not participants inside it — and whether she can carry that instruction with the news
Say nothing yet — let the problem sit until you have spoken with Thessaly Vorn and know what the Conclave's geometry actually requires
Ask Orrath from behind whether the Unsutured oral record gives the bands any standing at the fifth-point working — a precedent that Vorn could not dismiss as interference
Ask Davan directly: if she were framing their presence to the bands themselves, what would she tell them their purpose is
Stop walking for a moment and think aloud — the bands, Vorn, the three-source convergence, and forty-four days; let Orrath and Davan hear the planning as it forms

narrativeTurns.playerInput: Ask Davan whether the bands would accept a specific role — witnesses at the boundary of the ritual, not participants inside it — and whether she can carry that instruction with the news
narrativeTurns.narration:
The level stretch ends and the path narrows again — single file, the ravine wall close on your left, the drop away to the right. You wait until the ground opens slightly, then speak to Davan's back, or to the side of her face when she turns her head slightly to read the next footfall. The question is precise: not whether the bands will come, but whether they will hold a specific position — witnesses at the ritual boundary, outside the geometry, not participants inside it. A frame for their movement before the movement begins. And whether Davan can carry that instruction alongside the news of the column's stilling.

Davan is quiet for long enough that the path narrows and widens again. She does not answer until she has chosen the question fully, which you have come to understand is not reluctance — it is the shaman's care for an answer that will not need to be corrected later.

'Boundary-witnesses.' She weighs the compound word in trade-tongue the way she weighed sorveth-anak — for accuracy, not flavor. 'Yes. I can carry that.' A pause, stepping over a Fault-vein crossing, the amber light briefly warming the sole of her boot. 'The word in Thornwood for it is sorveth-tavan. Those who stand where the door is and do not go through. It is not a lesser role.' She says this without defensiveness — it is technical information. 'The bands will understand it as a position with its own weight. Not exclusion.' Another pause, shorter. 'The difficulty —' Her rough consonants tighten slightly. 'Is that I must bring them the word before they hear the news another way. If word of the column reaches them through traders or travelers first, they will have already decided to move. The frame needs to arrive with the news, not after it.'

Orrath, just behind you, says nothing. But his pace adjusts — fractionally, barely perceptible — the way a man adjusts when something he has been holding an edge of finally resolves. He has been waiting, you understand, to know whether Davan would leave the descent before the staging camp or after it. He now knows. So do you.

Davan speaks once more, without turning her head, her eyes on the path ahead. 'Two days to the first band from the King's Road. I can move faster alone than the three of us together.' A clean statement of fact. Not a request. Not a farewell yet. But the shape of one is in it, and the mountain carries it forward without ornamentation.
narrativeTurns.ask: Davan is proposing to split from you and Orrath at some point on the descent — moving faster alone to reach the first Thornwood band before word of the column arrives by other means. How do you respond, and when do you intend to part ways — at the staging camp, at the King's Road, or sooner?
suggestedActions: Release her now — the staging camp can wait; she should move at the mountain's pace, not yours
Ask her to stay through the staging camp so you can give her something to carry — a word, a token, or an instruction for Thessaly Vorn's wardens who may be watching the foothills
Ask what the sorveth-tavan role requires of the bands practically — how close to the ritual site, what they witness, and what they do if something goes wrong inside
Ask Orrath whether the Unsutured oral record says anything about witnesses at the boundary — whether sorveth-tavan has its own weight in the older texts
Say nothing and let Davan choose her own moment to break away — she has read every threshold correctly so far

narrativeTurns.playerInput: Release her now — the staging camp can wait; she should move at the mountain's pace, not yours
narrativeTurns.narration:
You stop on the path where it widens enough to turn — a flat shelf of granite, the ravine still visible below, the High Approach shelf visible above. You look at Davan and give a small nod toward the foothills, east and down, away from the King's Road line you and Orrath will take. The meaning is plain: go now, not at the staging camp, not at the road junction. The mountain's pace, not yours.

Davan reads it without requiring words. She stops walking and turns to face you fully — the same quality of attention she brought to the Greyveil threshold, to the column's edge, to every moment that required her full presence before she committed weight. She looks at you for one held breath. Then she looks at Orrath, briefly, the look of two people who have traveled the same road long enough that a glance carries a full conversation. Orrath gives nothing back — only the stillness of a man who agrees and does not need to say so.

She adjusts the pack across her shoulder — a single practical motion — and steps off the path onto the eastern line, the shaman's footing, native to terrain like this in the way yours is native to forms done in empty rooms. She does not say farewell. In Thornwood, you understand by now, departure and arrival are both continuations. But at the last moment before the rock-face angles between you, she turns her head and speaks, in her rough-consonant trade-tongue, without ceremony: 'Sorveth-tavan. I will give them the word before the news.' A pause, the foothills wind moving between you. 'And I will tell them the Wellspring is arriving.'

Then she is below the shelf and gone — not vanished, simply moving at the mountain's pace on the mountain's terms, the way she has moved since the milestone outside Veranthos when she was simply present without introduction. The path she takes is not on your route-map. It does not need to be.

Orrath stands beside you on the shelf. The silence between you has the quality of something correctly completed. After a moment he turns back to the upward path, and his footfall resumes — slow, reading the ground, the same pace as the whole descent. The staging camp is above. The King's Road is above. Thessaly Vorn and forty-three remaining days are above.
suggestedActions: Resume the ascent beside Orrath in silence — let the mountain hold what was just given
Walk beside Orrath and begin thinking aloud about how to open the conversation with Thessaly Vorn — test the three-source convergence argument on him before you reach her
Ask Orrath whether he has any prior knowledge of Thessaly Vorn — her character, her limits, what she will protect before she will hear
Move at your own pace up the path and simply observe what the Shard feels like now that Davan has separated — notice whether its quiet holds or shifts
Pause on the shelf a moment longer and watch the direction Davan took before turning back to the ascent

narrativeTurns.playerInput: Ask Orrath whether he has any prior knowledge of Thessaly Vorn — her character, her limits, what she will protect before she will hear
narrativeTurns.narration:
The path narrows again as you approach the High Approach shelf — the granite shoulder that will bring you back to the staging camp's sight-line. The afternoon light has shifted, the mountain's shadow beginning its slow westward lean. You and Orrath move in the easy silence of people who have said the necessary things and do not need to fill the interval. Then you ask it, without breaking stride: what he knows of Thessaly Vorn. Her character. Her limits. What she will protect before she will hear.

Orrath is quiet for long enough that you clear the narrow section and reach the broader shelf above. Then he speaks, in his weighted trade-tongue, eyes forward on the path.

'I have not met her.' He says this first, as a boundary — precision before the content. 'But the Unsutured have watched the Conclave's movements for eleven years from the foothills boundary. You learn a person from the shape of their orders.' A pause, footfall on loose stone, weight correctly placed. 'Her orders at the foothills were specific. Not cruel. Specific. No violence — she did not want a grievance in the record. But absolute — no passage, no dialogue, no exceptions for elders or shamans or anyone carrying the older knowledge.' He steps over a Fault-vein crossing and continues. 'The orders of a person who has already decided what the answer is and does not want the question reopened. Not from arrogance.' He considers the next word carefully. 'From fear. The kind of fear that has been load-bearing so long it has become a method.'

He is quiet again for several paces. Then: 'She has four shrine-components. She believes this is complete. Eleven years of believing something load-bearing — and the Clock is now at forty-four days.' He glances at you once, sideways, the rare direct look. 'A person in that position does not hear a new voice easily. She hears it as the thing that will make her wrong at the moment she cannot afford to be wrong.' A pause. 'Her limit is not intelligence. It is not cruelty. It is that she has been carrying the weight of everyone in that chamber for so long that protecting them and protecting her certainty have become the same motion. She cannot feel where one ends and the other begins.'

The staging camp's canvas appears at the path's next turn — a smear of pale against grey rock, distant but visible. Orrath sees it the same moment you do. He speaks once more, quieter, still forward-facing. 'Three-source convergence will not move her by itself. Geography does not move a person whose map is already drawn.' A pause. 'What might move her is if it arrives before she has publicly committed the ritual geometry. Before the circuit is announced as complete. After that point —' He does not finish the sentence. He does not need to. After that point, Thessaly Vorn hearing the truth and acting on it would require her to admit, in front of everyone watching the Clock, that she has been wrong for eleven years. That is a different conversation entirely — and possibly not a conversation at all.
narrativeTurns.ask: The staging camp is visible ahead. You have Orrath's reading of Vorn — her fear, her load-bearing certainty, the narrow window before she commits the geometry publicly. What do you do?
suggestedActions: Ask Orrath whether he knows the ritual geometry's announcement timeline — how many days before the Clock expires Vorn typically locks the circuit
Walk the rest of the way in silence and use the remaining distance to settle what you will say to Vorn when the moment comes
Ask Orrath whether the Conclave has any internal dissenters — someone inside the blue threadwork who already doubts the four-component frame
When you reach the staging camp, review the route-map and plan the King's Road descent — calculate how many days remain before you must stand in front of Vorn

narrativeTurns.playerInput: Sometimes you need to create leverage. I ask Orrath to keep going and I will wait until the late night. Break into the camp and take one of the shrine-components.
narrativeTurns.narration:
Orrath stops walking.

Not the reading-the-ground stop — the full stop, weight settled, the way he stops when something requires his whole attention. He turns to look at you. His face does not show alarm or judgment. It shows the thing beneath both: a shaman's recognition of a method that will cut the person who uses it.

'The staging camp has Conclave wardens,' he says. Not a warning. A fact. 'And a shrine-component does not feel like stone. It resonates.' A pause, the mountain wind moving between you. 'If you carry it away from its warding seal, the Sealers will know within the hour. They will not know who. But they will lock the others.' He holds your eyes. 'And then we arrive at Thessaly Vorn having already told her someone is working against the ritual.' He does not say: that closes the door before we reach it. He does not need to.

He reads something in your face — the calculation you have already run, the forty-four days, the load-bearing certainty he described on the way up — and he is quiet for a moment longer. Then: 'There is another kind of leverage.' He says it carefully, the way he says everything that matters. 'The Conclave does not know the ritual geometry is incomplete. They believe four components are sufficient. If that belief can be made to cost them something demonstrable — not stolen, not argued, but shown — then Vorn's map has a visible error and she must redraw it.' A pause. 'I do not yet know how to show her. But the showing leaves every door open. The taking closes them all.'

You are on the High Approach shelf with the staging camp visible ahead and forty-four days on a clock Thessaly Vorn believes she has answered. The shrine-component is there — canvas and warding seal and wardens who do not yet know you exist. The night is coming on.
suggestedActions: Hear Orrath out — ask what showing her the error could look like in practice before the ritual geometry is committed
Hold your plan but adapt it: not theft, but a closer look at the component and its warding seal tonight — reconnaissance only
Acknowledge the risk and set the plan aside — press forward to the staging camp and begin the harder conversation
Ask Orrath what he knows of Serevaine and the Grey-Faction — whether they have leverage of the kind you are looking for

diceResult.reason: Infiltrating a guarded staging camp at night to steal a consecrated ritual component is high-risk and uncertain. Success requires stealth, timing, and avoiding detection—a Hard check reflecting the danger and skill required.

narrativeTurns.playerInput: I said to Orrath: Your kindness and wisdom are like the mountains. But her door is never opened. Did she ever listen to your people when she makes any decision? The only price to make them learn is after they've done the wrong ritual. Tell me what will happen if the wrong ritual is performed.
narrativeTurns.narration:
The words land cleanly, the way a spear-throw lands when the release is honest. Orrath receives them without deflection — he does not perform modesty at the compliment, he does not flinch from the logic underneath it. You are watching him think with his whole body, the way he does everything, and you can read from the set of his shoulders that you have named something he has been carrying alongside the three-source convergence argument without letting himself say it aloud.

He is quiet for a long stretch of path. The staging camp is closer now — you can make out the canvas shapes, the pale smear of Conclave blue-threadwork on the tent-lines, two warden-shapes moving at the perimeter. The light has gone amber and lateral, the mountain shadow beginning its long lean. Then Orrath speaks, slowly, in the weighted trade-tongue that means he is translating not just language but register.

'She has not listened.' Flat. Not bitter — accurate. 'The Unsutured sent three delegations in eleven years. The first was turned back at the foothills boundary with courtesy. The second with wardens. The third did not return with the same people who left.' A pause. His hands are loose at his sides. 'We do not know what she told herself about us. Probably that we were Quietists in different clothing. She needed us to be a known thing.' He steps over a Fault-vein crossing and continues. 'What happens if the wrong ritual is performed.' He says it as a restatement, not a question — the shaman's habit of naming the question clearly before attempting the answer. And here is where you see it: the partial edge of what you reached for. You have the shape of the consequence from Serevaine's knowledge, from the Wellspring's own transmission, from the logic of an unanswered circuit closing around the wrong frequency — but the precise Unsutured account of what rupture does to a chamber, to a city, to the land between the Auric Mountains and Veranthos, that technical knowledge does not come fully. What Orrath carries and what you have already received do not yet articulate the same interior. You have the outline. Not the weight.

Orrath's answer fills some of it. 'The circuit closes,' he says, 'around the shape the Sealers believe is there. The old shape. What the Wellspring was before the Covenant. That shape has not existed for a thousand years.' He stops walking for a moment — not to rest, to name this correctly. 'A circuit closed around a shape that is not present does not hold. It does not hold because there is nothing to hold against. The geometry collapses inward. The Wellspring is not sealed — it is cut.' He resumes walking. 'What a Wellspring does when it is cut is not the same as what it does when it ruptures naturally. A rupture spreads outward slowly — the Sundering Clock was built for that kind of dying. A cut —' He stops again. Briefer pause this time. 'The Unsutured word for it has no trade-tongue parallel. The closest is: a thing that was deciding what it is, told violently that it is nothing. The Fault-scars would not bleed warmth. They would open.' He looks at you directly. 'Veranthos is built on Fault-scar country. The city, the King's Road, the foothills. The bands sheltering there.' A pause. 'Forty-four days of Sundering Clock would become something closer to four.' He does not say: and everyone in the ritual chamber would die before the city did. He does not need to. You can read it in the geography he has just described.

The staging camp is a hundred yards ahead. Lantern-blue is beginning to glow in the tent-lines as the light fails. Somewhere in there, four shrine-components sit in their warding seals, and Thessaly Vorn believes the geometry is complete.
narrativeTurns.ask: The door to Vorn has never been opened to the Unsutured, and you now carry the weight of what a wrong ritual means for Veranthos and everything between it and the mountains. What do you do as you approach the staging camp?
suggestedActions: Enter the staging camp directly and request an immediate audience with whoever leads it — a first contact, reading the camp's disposition before deciding how much to reveal
Stop outside the camp perimeter and observe — count the wardens, read the layout, find where the shrine-components are warded before stepping inside
Ask Orrath whether any Conclave Sealers in this camp are known to the Unsutured as someone other than Vorn's instrument — a dissenter, a doubter, someone whose map is not yet fully drawn
Enter the camp as a traveler seeking lodging for the night — establish presence without revealing purpose; let the camp show you what it is before you show it what you carry
Make camp outside the staging site, away from Conclave sight, and wait for first light — the conversation with Vorn deserves a full day, not the edge of an exhausted evening

diceResult.reason: Persuasion with social resistance—Orrath has already redirected the plan once. Player is attempting to reframe the moral/practical argument and extract information that could shift Orrath's stance. Outcome is uncertain; success/failure meaningfully affects group dynamics and next moves.

imagePrompt: High fantasy illustration. A lean Far-Eastern man — weathered face, dark eyes, foreign-cut travelling coat, spear-callus visible on his bare right hand but no weapon at his side — stands at the edge of a mountain staging camp at dusk, the tents lit from within by cold lantern-blue light. Behind him the vast ravine drops away into amber Fault-vein glow, the distant voreth-column still faintly turning in the deep dark below. Beside him an older Thornwood shaman in layered mountain gear stands with weight-settled stillness, both men looking toward the illuminated camp. The sky above the peaks is the deep violet of last light. The mood is urgent and morally heavy: the warmth of what was resolved below is behind them; what is ahead is lit in the cold blue of a certainty that does not yet know it is wrong. Characters depicted — Ming (Lean Far-Eastern man, weathered, dark eyes, foreign-cut travelling coat, spear-callus on the right palm but no weapon; moves with the economy of a martial discipline.); Orrath (Old as mapped terrain, face weathered deep, Thornwood clan-braid, heavy woven cloak, tends fire with a short stick). Illustration for a High Fantasy story (Epic, urgent, morally complex — heroism is possible but costly). Cinematic, atmospheric, consistent character appearance.

durableFacts.text: Ming received the full Shard transmission at the High Approach. The Wellspring's nature is now known to him: grief at the sealing, a thousand years of private dreaming, and gladness at the return of an exile. It is utharvel. He knows what it is but not yet what it wills.

The Opened Ear: a natural granite shelf on the Auric Mountain mid-climb, reached after the first granite shoulder. From here the ravine and a slow-turning column of voreth-light descending from an unlocatable high point are fully visible. The Shard recognises this as a terminus — seventeen years of orientation resolved. The path continues down into the ravine toward the column's base.

Ming has reached the ravine floor at the base of the voreth-light column — the Wellspring's physical presence point beneath the Auric Mountains. The column turns slowly. Its densest point is at sternum height. The Shard is at its warmest and most oriented. The Wellspring is waiting.

Ming entered the voreth-light column. The Wellspring made direct contact — not transmission but presence. It asked its first question: not whether Ming will offer, but whether he understands what he is witnessing before he chooses. This is the Wellspring's will making itself known for the first time in a thousand years.

Ming performed the Crane-in-Storm form inside the voreth-light column. Partial knowing received: witnessing is not neutrality — contact changes the ground that the form moves through. The Wellspring's question is understood but not yet fully answered. The final layer — what it wills for its dreaming once witnessed — has not arrived.

Ming received the Wellspring's full will inside the voreth-light column. It wills to be sealed as what it now is — utharvel, the third thing. The fifth-point requires testimony from a living will, not a key or an offering: the vessel must declare the Wellspring's true present shape to the re-binding geometry. The voreth-light stilled. The Shard is at rest. The Wellspring has been fully witnessed.

Ming has exited the voreth-light column and spoken the Wellspring's will plainly to Orrath and Davan: testimony not offering, utharvel sealed as it now is, vessel survival unresolved. He has asked Orrath whether the Unsutured's eleven-year position anticipated the testimony-not-offering distinction.

Davan confirmed the Unsutured eleven-year position anticipated testimony-not-offering. The Thornwood word is sorveth-anak — the one who speaks-what-is-true-at-the-closing. Whether this role persists inside the closed circuit or is consumed by it remains unanswered; the eleven years held the distinction, not the solution. The name Sorveth is Thornwood for 'the one who built the frame around the door' — Liang Sorveth knew the distinction and sent Ming with the frame, not the answer.

Liang Sorveth's third text and carved name-token are buried in a Fault-vein crack at the base of the voreth-light column in the Auric Mountain ravine. Ming placed them deliberately — the frame returned to the ground. They are no longer in his possession.

Ming has articulated the sorveth-anak function: a living harmony — the Wellspring's true present frequency spoken into the Conclave's re-binding geometry at the moment of closing. The circuit must close around what is actually there. Thessaly Vorn does not know this distinction exists. 44 days remain.

Orrath confirmed the Unsutured held sorveth-anak distinct from the one who dies because collapsing the two words would mean the Wellspring receives a death at its closing, not testimony. The distinction is the reason the eleven years existed.
Davan stated that in Thornwood, the name Sorveth means a person who knows the door must be found and cannot lead you through it — a form of love, not condemnation. The frame held.

Orrath has named the rhetorical frame for presenting the sorveth-anak distinction to Thessaly Vorn: three-source convergence from roads that did not know each other — Sorveth's text, Unsutured position, Wellspring's own declaration. Geography, not argument.

Davan has identified Thornwood bands sheltering in the Auric Mountain foothills as a potential resource. They will want to know the Opened Ear received a witness and the Wellspring is arriving, not dying. Their response is unknown.

The Unsutured oral record, older than the Covenant, described the Prior Shard vessel before Ming arrived: exiled, unknowing, would arrive at the column speaking the language of their first home. Orrath withheld this on the road because the record forbids leading the vessel. Orrath and Sorveth held the same instruction from different sides of the same text without knowing the other existed.

Davan has separated from Ming and Orrath on the mid-ascent path to move independently to the Thornwood foothills bands. She carries the sorveth-tavan frame and the news of the column's stilling. She will reach the first band in approximately two days. Ming and Orrath continue to the staging camp alone.

Shrine-components in the staging camp are warded: removing one from its seal triggers Conclave awareness within the hour and causes them to lock the remaining components. Theft would close the door to Thessaly Vorn before it is opened. Plan not executed.

Orrath has described the consequence of an incorrect re-binding: circuit closes around absent shape, geometry collapses inward, Fault-scars open rather than bleed — Sundering accelerates from 44 days to approximately 4. The Unsutured have no delegation access to Thessaly Vorn; three prior delegations were refused, the third losing members.
